title: Two Pointers
description: Using two index variables moving through an array to solve problems in O(n) instead of the O(n^2) brute force from Big-O Notation.
objectives: Recognize when a two-pointer approach applies
Use opposite-direction pointers to solve a pair-sum problem
Compare the two-pointer O(n) solution against a brute-force O(n^2) one

sections.text: 1. The Problem: Brute Force is O(n^2)
Suppose we have a SORTED array and want to find two numbers that add up to a target. The obvious approach: check every pair.
This is O(n^2), exactly like the hasDuplicate example from Big-O Notation -- nested loops checking every pair. For a sorted array, we can do dramatically better.

A technique using two index variables moving through a data structure -- often from opposite ends inward, or both forward at different speeds -- to solve a problem in a single pass instead of nested loops.

sections.title: Tracing hasPairTwoPointers({1,3,5,7,9,11}, 12)
steps: left=0 (value 1), right=5 (value 11): sum=12 -- MATCH, return true immediately

sections.title: Tracing hasPairTwoPointers({1,3,5,7,9,11}, 25) -- no pair sums this high
steps: left=0 (1), right=5 (11): sum=12 < 25 -- too small, move left forward: left=1
left=1 (3), right=5 (11): sum=14 < 25 -- move left forward: left=2
left=2 (5), right=5 (11): sum=16 < 25 -- move left forward: left=3
left=3 (7), right=5 (11): sum=18 < 25 -- move left forward: left=4
left=4 (9), right=5 (11): sum=20 < 25 -- move left forward: left=5
left=5, right=5: left < right is now false -- loop ends, return false

sections.text: This algorithm ONLY works because the array is SORTED. If sum is too small, moving left forward can only increase it (since values increase left to right); if sum is too large, moving right backward can only decrease it. This directional guarantee is exactly what an unsorted array would break.

sections.title: Why two pointers beats brute force here
points: left only ever moves forward, right only ever moves backward
Combined, they can move AT MOST n times total before crossing (left >= right)
One single pass through the array, O(n), instead of checking every pair, O(n^2)

sections.text: 4. Removing Duplicates from a Sorted Array (Same-Direction Pointers)

This is a DIFFERENT two-pointer pattern: slow and fast both move forward, but at different rates. fast scans every element; slow only advances (and overwrites arr[slow]) when a genuinely new value is found. This modifies the array in place, achieving O(n) with no extra array needed.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Using the opposite-direction two-pointer pattern on an UNSORTED array -- the technique relies entirely on sorted order to know which pointer to move.
Forgetting the loop condition should be left < right (not <=) for opposite-direction pointers, to avoid comparing an element with itself or crossing incorrectly.
Confusing the two different two-pointer patterns -- opposite-direction (converging inward) vs same-direction at different speeds (slow/fast) -- they solve different kinds of problems.
Reaching for nested loops out of habit when a sorted array and a two-pointer approach would solve the same problem in O(n) instead of O(n^2).

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is the two-pointer technique?
questions.answer: An approach using two index variables moving through a data structure -- either converging from opposite ends, or moving in the same direction at different speeds -- to solve a problem in a single O(n) pass instead of nested O(n^2) loops.

questions.question: Why does the opposite-direction two-pointer pair-sum approach require a sorted array?
questions.answer: Because moving left forward only increases the sum (since values increase left to right in sorted order), and moving right backward only decreases it -- this directional guarantee is what lets the algorithm safely decide which pointer to move at each step.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of the opposite-direction two-pointer approach, and why?
questions.answer: O(n). left only ever moves forward and right only ever moves backward, so together they can move at most n times total before the pointers cross, giving a single overall pass through the array.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Two pointers uses two index variables moving through an array to avoid nested loops. One common pattern converges two pointers from opposite ends inward -- for pair-sum on a SORTED array, moving the left pointer forward increases the sum and moving the right pointer backward decreases it, letting the algorithm solve the problem in O(n) instead of the brute-force O(n^2). Another pattern moves two pointers in the same direction at different speeds (slow/fast), useful for tasks like removing duplicates from a sorted array in place. Both patterns replace nested-loop brute force with a single efficient pass.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: Two pointers = two hands reaching into a sorted line from both ends, walking toward each other, OR one hand scouting ahead (fast) while the other marks progress (slow). Either way: one honest pass, O(n), no nested loops needed.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: Why does the opposite-direction pair-sum technique require a sorted array?
What is the time complexity of the two-pointer pair-sum approach?
What's the difference between the opposite-direction and same-direction (slow/fast) two-pointer patterns?
What would happen if you used the pair-sum two-pointer technique on an unsorted array?

sections.title: Two Sum II - Input Array Is Sorted
sections.problemStatement: Given a 1-indexed array of integers numbers sorted in non-decreasing order, find two numbers that add up to target. Return the indices (1-indexed) as an array [index1, index2]. You may assume exactly one solution exists.
constraints: numbers is sorted in non-decreasing order
hints: This is the exact opposite-direction two-pointer pattern from this lesson's Section 2 -- the array being sorted is what makes it work.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Check every pair with nested loops.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Two pointers converging from opposite ends, exactly as shown in this lesson's Section 2.

sections.title: Valid Palindrome
sections.problemStatement: Given a string s, return true if it is a palindrome after converting all uppercase letters to lowercase and removing all non-alphanumeric characters.

examples.input: s = "A man, a plan, a canal: Panama"

constraints: s consists only of printable ASCII characters
hints: Two pointers converging from both ends of the STRING, skipping non-alphanumeric characters as they go, and comparing lowercase letters.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Build a cleaned (lowercase, alphanumeric-only) String first, then compare it to its own reverse.
approaches.output: For s = "A man, a plan, a canal: Panama": true
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) for the cleaned String copies

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Two pointers from both ends of the original string, skipping non-alphanumeric characters in place -- no extra String copies needed.
approaches.output: For s = "A man, a plan, a canal: Panama": true

sections.title: Move Zeroes
sections.problemStatement: Given an integer array nums, move all 0's to the end while maintaining the relative order of the non-zero elements. This must be done in place without making a copy of the array.
hints: This is the same-direction slow/fast two-pointer pattern from this lesson's removeDuplicates example -- slow marks the next position for a non-zero element.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Build a new list of non-zero elements, then overwrite nums with them followed by zeros.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Same-direction slow/fast pointers, in place -- slow tracks where the next non-zero should go, fast scans ahead.

sections.title: Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array
sections.problemStatement: Given an integer array nums sorted in non-decreasing order, remove duplicates in place such that each unique element appears only once. Return the number of unique elements k; the first k elements of nums should hold the final result.

examples.explanation: 2 unique elements, in the first two positions.

constraints: nums is sorted in non-decreasing order
hints: This is exactly this lesson's removeDuplicates example -- slow/fast pointers, moving forward at different rates.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Build a separate list of unique values, then copy them back into nums.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: In-place slow/fast pointers, identical to this lesson's own removeDuplicates example.

sections.title: Remove Element
sections.problemStatement: Given an array nums and an integer val, remove all occurrences of val in place. Return the number of elements not equal to val (k); the first k elements should hold those values, in any order.
hints: Same slow/fast pointer shape as Remove Duplicates, but the condition to keep an element is nums[fast] != val instead of a duplicate check.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Build a list of elements that aren't val, then copy them back into nums.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: In-place slow/fast pointers -- slow marks the next position for a kept element.

sections.title: Squares of a Sorted Array
sections.problemStatement: Given an integer array nums sorted in non-decreasing order, return an array of the squares of each number, also sorted in non-decreasing order.
constraints: nums is sorted in non-decreasing order
hints: The LARGEST square always comes from whichever end (leftmost or rightmost) has the greater absolute value -- fill the result from the back using two pointers converging inward.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Square every element, then sort the result.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n log n) due to sorting
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) for the output (sort itself uses O(log n) auxiliary for primitives)

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Two pointers from both ends, comparing absolute values, filling the result array from the back forward.

sections.title: Container With Most Water
sections.problemStatement: Given n non-negative integers height[i] representing vertical lines, find two lines that together with the x-axis form a container that holds the most water. Return the maximum amount of water it can store.

examples.explanation: The lines at index 1 (height 8) and index 8 (height 7) form a container of width 7 and height min(8,7)=7, area 49.

hints: Start with the widest possible container (both ends). The SHORTER of the two lines is always the bottleneck -- moving that pointer inward is the only way a better area could be found.
Moving the taller pointer inward can never help, since width shrinks and the height is still capped by the shorter line.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Check every pair of lines and compute the area, tracking the maximum.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Two pointers from both ends, always moving the pointer at the SHORTER line inward, since it's the bottleneck.

sections.title: 3Sum
sections.problemStatement: Given an integer array nums, return all unique triplets [nums[i], nums[j], nums[k]] such that i != j != k and nums[i] + nums[j] + nums[k] == 0. The solution set must not contain duplicate triplets.
hints: Sort the array first, then FIX one number and use the opposite-direction Two Pointers technique to find pairs summing to its negation.
Skip over duplicate values at each level (fixed number and both pointers) to avoid duplicate triplets in the result.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Check every triplet with three nested loops, using a Set to avoid duplicate results.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Sort first, then fix each number and use opposite-direction two pointers on the rest, skipping duplicates at every level.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) for sorting and the result

sections.title: Trapping Rain Water
sections.problemStatement: Given n non-negative integers representing an elevation map where the width of each bar is 1, compute how much water it can trap after raining.
hints: Water trapped above any index i is min(maxLeft of i, maxRight of i) - height[i].
Two pointers from both ends, tracking the running max seen from each side, can compute this in a single pass without precomputing full maxLeft/maxRight arrays.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For every index, scan left and right separately to find the max height on each side.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Two pointers from both ends with running maxLeft/maxRight trackers -- whichever side has the smaller running max determines how much water is trapped at that pointer's position.
